critic_raw:
This is a carefully written experimental design with several genuine strengths: the conservative double-confirmation labeling rule is sound, the scope limitations are honestly stated, and the stratified subgroup evaluation is thoughtful. However, I identify three genuine methodological flaws, one of which is severe enough to undermine the core comparison.

**Issue 1: Temporal leakage in graph features — the most serious flaw**

The claim: Graph statistics (sender in-degree, out-degree, reputation, first-time-sender flag) are computed once from the 'development corpus' and then frozen. The same frozen features are used identically across train, validation, and test messages.

Why this is wrong: The 'development corpus' is described as the full 18-month inbound log. If graph statistics are computed over all 18 months before any split occurs, then a message arriving in month 3 will have its sender reputation computed using signal from months 4–18, including signal about phishing campaigns that hadn't yet occurred when the message was received. More concretely, a campaign that sent 50 messages in month 3 (all unlabeled at send time) and then got confirmed malicious in month 15 will appear in the graph with non-zero reputation at training time — a feature that would be zero or near-zero in any honest production deployment. This inflates the informativeness of the reputation feature during evaluation, and because the test set also gets graph features computed with full-corpus look-ahead, the reported test ROC-AUC reflects a privileged signal that doesn't exist at inference time. The 'frozen graph' framing sounds methodologically rigorous but is actually describing a temporally contaminated feature, not a safe one. The first-time-sender flag is similarly affected: a domain that first appeared in month 16 would be incorrectly flagged as 'established' in a correctly time-split graph, but correctly flagged as 'new' in the contaminated one — changing the feature value and concealing the difficulty of detecting novel senders.

What would constitute evidence: Re-run the feature construction using only messages with arrival timestamps strictly earlier than each split boundary. If sender reputation and first-time-sender flag computed this way (per-message, using only historical signal available at that message's arrival time) yield substantially different feature distributions on the test set, the contamination is real and the AUC difference between joint and text-only models is unreliable. A formal check: compute the fraction of test-set senders that have nonzero reputation in the frozen graph but zero reputation in the time-respecting graph.

**Issue 2: Asymmetric hyperparameter search between joint model and baseline creates a confounded comparison**

The claim: The baseline uses 'one standard fine-tuning configuration with fixed learning rate and dropout' while the joint model receives a 'full hyperparameter search.'

Why this is wrong: Any AUC difference between the two models conflates the contribution of graph features with the contribution of additional tuning. The joint model has more degrees of freedom to exploit (both in architecture and in search), so it will, on average, find a better-calibrated checkpoint. The correct scientific question is: given identically optimized models that differ only in whether graph features are present, does graph context improve separability? The asymmetric search means the experiment as described does not answer that question — it answers the muddier question of whether a tuned joint model beats an untuned text-only model. This is particularly problematic because the validation ROC-AUC checkpoint selection procedure will favor the joint model simply because it has had more optimization pressure applied.

What would constitute evidence: Run the full hyperparameter search on both models, or fix identical hyperparameters for both. If the advantage of the joint model disappears or shrinks substantially when search budgets are equalized, the original advantage was attributable to search asymmetry rather than graph signal.

**Issue 3: Message-level stratified split induces campaign-level leakage**

The claim: Stratified sampling at the message level preserves label prevalence and sender-domain rarity while controlling for month composition.

Why this is wrong: Enterprise phishing campaigns typically send near-identical or identical messages (same subject template, same body, same sending domain) to many recipients over a short window. Message-level stratification with coarse month-of-arrival controls will split these near-duplicate messages across train and test. The transformer text encoder will then 'memorize' the template on training examples and recognize it nearly perfectly on test examples, inflating ROC-AUC in a way that doesn't reflect the model's ability to detect novel campaigns. The subgroup metric on 'new sender domains' partially addresses this, but sender domain uniqueness doesn't imply message content uniqueness — a campaign may use a new domain with a copy-paste body template. The correct split unit for phishing detection is the campaign or template cluster, not the individual message.

What would constitute evidence: Apply near-duplicate detection (e.g., MinHash LSH on body text) to identify message clusters, then perform the train/test split at the cluster level. Compare ROC-AUC under cluster-level splits vs. the message-level splits used here. If AUC drops meaningfully under cluster-level splits, the current evaluation is inflated by campaign memorization.

**Summary:** Issues 1 and 2 are genuine methodological flaws. Issue 1 (temporal leakage in graph features) is severe — it corrupts the primary signal being tested and means the reported AUC advantage of the joint model cannot be attributed to the causal mechanism the experiment claims to test. Issue 2 (asymmetric search) means any remaining advantage is partially confounded with optimization budget. Issue 3 is real but partially mitigated by the month-slice stratification and domain-level subgroup analysis; it is a meaningful concern but less disqualifying than issues 1 and 2.
all_issues_raised: Graph features (especially sender reputation and first-time-sender flag) are computed over the full 18-month corpus before splitting, introducing temporal look-ahead leakage that inflates the joint model's AUC beyond what would be achievable at deployment time.

The joint model receives a full hyperparameter search while the baseline uses a fixed configuration, confounding the model comparison so that any measured AUC difference reflects both graph signal and optimization advantage.

Message-level stratified splitting allows near-duplicate phishing campaign messages to appear in both train and test, inflating ROC-AUC by enabling template memorization rather than measuring generalization to novel attacks.
all_issues_adjudicated: Graph features (especially sender reputation and first-time-sender flag) are computed over the full 18-month corpus before splitting, introducing temporal look-ahead leakage that inflates the joint model's AUC beyond what would be achievable at deployment time.

The joint model receives a full hyperparameter search while the baseline uses a fixed configuration, confounding the model comparison so that any measured AUC difference reflects both graph signal and optimization advantage.

Message-level stratified splitting allows near-duplicate phishing campaign messages to appear in both train and test, inflating ROC-AUC by enabling template memorization rather than measuring generalization to novel attacks.
